scenario: You open from CO, BB calls. Flop K‚ô†7‚ô•2‚ô£. You hold A‚ô£K‚ô¶ (top pair, ace kicker). BB checks to you.
question: Which Hardin hand-strength category is this?
explanation: Top pair top kicker on a dry board is STRONG VALUE. Default: bet for value across multiple streets ‚Äî value betting is where most micro-stakes profit comes from. You want worse Kx, pairs, and draws to pay you off. (You're the aggressor in position here, so you can bet all three streets comfortably.)

scenario: You open from MP, BTN calls. Flop J‚ô¶8‚ô†5‚ô•. You hold J‚ô†9‚ô£ (top pair, NINE kicker). Turn 2‚ô£. A tight BTN has called once and you're first to act.
question: Category for top pair with a weak kicker vs a tight caller?
explanation: Top pair with a WEAK kicker (the 9) is MEDIUM STRENGTH ‚Äî it has showdown value but is dominated by better Jx. Default: pot control ‚Äî check, or thin-value bet only vs weaker opponents. Against a tight caller whose range is firming up, don't bloat the pot with a one-pair, weak-kicker hand. (Note: if your kicker were an A or K, this would be top pair TOP kicker = Strong value, a different category. The kicker is what splits strong from medium here.)

scenario: You open from BTN, BB calls. Flop T‚ô†9‚ô¶4‚ô£. You hold Q‚ô†J‚ô† ‚Äî an open-ended straight draw (any K or 8 makes it) + backdoor flush + two overcards. BB checks.
question: Category and default plan with a strong draw in position?
options: Draw ‚Äî semi-bluff with the equity behind it
explanation: This is a DRAW ‚Äî and a strong one (8 straight outs + two overcards + backdoor flush). Default: semi-bluff. You have real equity if called AND fold equity now, and you can barrel many turns. As the in-position aggressor, betting is better than checking: you can win it now or improve. This is the 'draw upgrades you' principle ‚Äî bet, don't check.

scenario: You open from CO, a loose-passive 'calling station' in the BB calls. Flop K‚ô£9‚ô†4‚ô¶. You hold A‚ô•5‚ô• ‚Äî ace-high, no pair, no draw. The station checks to you.
question: Category and best line against a calling station?
options: Weak ‚Äî check/give up, don't fire at a station
explanation: Ace-high with no pair and no draw is WEAK. A weak-hand bluff only works vs players who FOLD ‚Äî and a calling station never folds. Firing here is lighting money on fire; you have no fold equity and no showdown value worth protecting. Check and give up unless you improve. THE PLAYER TYPE determines the line: vs a high-fold-to-c-bet reg you could bluff once, but never bluff a station.

scenario: YOUR HAND. Villain (a TAG) 3-bets preflop, you CALL with K‚ô†T‚ô† (you're the caller). Flop 6‚ô¶Q‚ô£J‚ô† ‚Äî you have K-high with an OPEN-ENDED straight draw: any A makes Broadway (A-K-Q-J-T) and any 9 makes K-Q-J-T-9. Villain bets.
question: Honestly categorize K-high + an 8-out open-ender vs a 3-bettor's range.
options: Weak ‚Äî only a few outs, fold
Draw ‚Äî 8 outs (any A or 9) + an overcard; enough equity to continue
explanation: This is a DRAW, and a real one: any A (4) or any 9 (4) completes a straight = 8 outs, ~31% by the river, plus the K is an overcard with some additional outs. That's NOT a weak 4-out gutshot ‚Äî it's an open-ended draw with enough equity to continue (call or, depending on price and fold equity, raise as a semi-bluff). As the CALLER out of position vs a 3-bettor you must respect their strong range, but 8 outs + overcard is a continue, not a fold. (Correcting an earlier version of this card that miscounted it as a 4-out gutshot ‚Äî it's 8 outs.)

scenario: You open from BTN, BB calls (you're the aggressor in position). Flop K‚ô¶Q‚ô†5‚ô£, BB check-calls. Turn 3‚ô†, BB check-calls again. River 2‚ô• ‚Äî and now BB suddenly LEADS big into you. You hold K‚ô£J‚ô† (top pair, J kicker).
question: BB check-called twice then leads big on the river. Re-categorize your hand NOW.
options: Bluff-catcher at best ‚Äî a passive player who leads after calling twice has KQ, sets, two pair; lean fold
explanation: Hand strength is DYNAMIC ‚Äî it changes street by street with the action. KJ (top pair, J kicker) was decent on the flop, but a passive player who calls twice then suddenly leads big has upgraded to a range that beats one pair: KQ (two pair), sets, slowplayed monsters. Your top-pair-decent-kicker demotes to a bluff-catcher, and passive players rarely turn a made hand into a bluff. Lean fold to the big lead. The ACTION redefines the category.

scenario: You open from MP, BTN calls (you're the aggressor in position). Flop 8‚ô•4‚ô£2‚ô¶ ‚Äî you hold 8‚ô†8‚ô¶, a flopped set of eights on a dry board. BTN checks.
question: Category and default plan with a set on a dry board?
options: Strong value (monster) ‚Äî build the pot toward stacks; occasionally slowplay one street on a very dry board
explanation: A set is a STRONG VALUE monster. Default: bet and build the pot toward getting stacks in. On a very dry board (8-4-2 rainbow, no draws) you can occasionally slowplay ONE street to let them catch up or bluff, since nothing realistically outdraws you cheaply ‚Äî but the default is still to build the pot. Don't get fancy and check it down.

scenario: YOUR HAND. Villain 4-bets preflop and you CALL with A‚ô£Q‚ô£ (you're the caller, vs a tight QQ+/AK 4-bet range). Flop K-8-4 checks through. Turn (a blank), villain bets, you call. River pairs the board; you BET, and villain shows QQ.
question: Categorize your A-Q on K-8-4 vs a 4-bettor's range ‚Äî and name the leak.
options: Weak vs this range ‚Äî A-high beats nothing a 4-bettor continues with; betting the river only folds out worse and gets called by better
explanation: A-Q on K-8-4 is just ace-high ‚Äî and vs a tight 4-bet range (QQ+/AK) it's WEAK: it beats nothing villain continues with (they have KK, AA, AK, QQ ‚Äî all of which beat or dominate you). Two leaks here: (1) the river BET turns ace-high into a bluff that only worse hands fold to and only better hands call ‚Äî a pure spew; and (2) the deeper leak is PREFLOP ‚Äî flat-calling a 4-bet with AQ is itself questionable, since vs a QQ+/AK 4-bet range you're dominated. You were bound to lose at showdown with ace-high; the fix is folding AQ to the 4-bet, or at minimum giving up postflop instead of betting. Honest read: this hand was a preflop leak that compounded postflop.
